MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to the directly relevant prior systems (Mishra et al. variants) on the same datasets and metrics and includes sensible ablations (text-only CNN vs. text+gaze), and it explicitly acknowledges when the gaze addition is statistically insignificant for sarcasm. However, from a methodological standpoint it appears to omit comparisons to stronger recent neural/text-only baselines beyond their own CNN and the Mishra handcrafted systems, and it relies on expensive gaze signals without broader demonstrations of benefit—hence the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against directly relevant prior systems that use handcrafted text+gaze features (Mishra et al. 2016 variants and Joshi et al. 2014) on the same datasets and metric (accuracy), and explicitly reports when gains are small or statistically insignificant (e.g., the 1.36% gaze addition for sarcasm). The authors also show CNN-only and CNN+gaze variants and report sizable wins (up to 11.27%) in some settings, which supports a fair head-to-head comparison. A minor omission is lack of broader contemporary neural baselines or ablations (e.g., other deep text-only architectures) and more thorough hyperparameter tuning, so the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to the directly relevant prior systems (Mishra et al. variants) on the same datasets and metrics and includes sensible ablations (text-only CNN vs. text+gaze), and it explicitly acknowledges when the gaze addition is statistically insignificant for sarcasm. However, from a methodological standpoint it appears to omit comparisons to stronger recent neural/text-only baselines beyond their own CNN and the Mishra handcrafted systems, and it relies on expensive gaze signals without broader demonstrations of benefit—hence the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against directly relevant prior systems that use handcrafted text+gaze features (Mishra et al. 2016 variants and Joshi et al. 2014) on the same datasets and metric (accuracy), and explicitly reports when gains are small or statistically insignificant (e.g., the 1.36% gaze addition for sarcasm). The authors also show CNN-only and CNN+gaze variants and report sizable wins (up to 11.27%) in some settings, which supports a fair head-to-head comparison. A minor omission is lack of broader contemporary neural baselines or ablations (e.g., other deep text-only architectures) and more thorough hyperparameter tuning, so the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments show some gains and compare to relevant handcrafted-gaze baselines, but key concerns remain: the reported improvement from adding gaze to the CNN for sarcasm is only 1.36% and statistically insignificant, yet no deeper analysis explains when or why gaze helps. Ablations and sensitivity analyses appear limited (no systematic ablation of gaze components, reader-aggregation strategies, or hyperparameter searches beyond trial-and-error). Related work coverage and an explicit discussion of limitations are adequate, but reproducibility is weak (code not mentioned and several methodological choices lack principled justification), and the paper depends on costly eye-tracking signals without demonstrating clear, repeatable benefit. | B(3): The experiments are adequate to show that the CNN learns useful representations (datasets with multiple readers, comparisons to prior handcrafted-feature baselines, and reported hyperparameters), but they lack deeper analyses I value for cognitive-signal work: no mention of systematic ablations isolating the gaze component across architectures, limited sensitivity analysis of hyperparameters, and no code release noted for exact reproducibility. Related work is described as adequate and some negative results/limitations (insignificant gaze gain for sarcasm) are acknowledged, but the paper stops short of thorough failure-case analysis or exploratory experiments that would clarify when and why gaze helps. Given these strengths and gaps, the empirical evaluation is solid but not comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments show some gains and compare to relevant handcrafted-gaze baselines, but key concerns remain: the reported improvement from adding gaze to the CNN for sarcasm is only 1.36% and statistically insignificant, yet no deeper analysis explains when or why gaze helps. Ablations and sensitivity analyses appear limited (no systematic ablation of gaze components, reader-aggregation strategies, or hyperparameter searches beyond trial-and-error). Related work coverage and an explicit discussion of limitations are adequate, but reproducibility is weak (code not mentioned and several methodological choices lack principled justification), and the paper depends on costly eye-tracking signals without demonstrating clear, repeatable benefit.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are adequate to show that the CNN learns useful representations (datasets with multiple readers, comparisons to prior handcrafted-feature baselines, and reported hyperparameters), but they lack deeper analyses I value for cognitive-signal work: no mention of systematic ablations isolating the gaze component across architectures, limited sensitivity analysis of hyperparameters, and no code release noted for exact reproducibility. Related work is described as adequate and some negative results/limitations (insignificant gaze gain for sarcasm) are acknowledged, but the paper stops short of thorough failure-case analysis or exploratory experiments that would clarify when and why gaze helps. Given these strengths and gaps, the empirical evaluation is solid but not comprehensive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies a standard CNN architecture to jointly learn representations from text and eye‑movement (gaze) signals, which is a logical extension of prior handcrafted‑feature work (Mishra et al.) rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. It does clearly differentiate itself from prior work by replacing handcrafted gaze/text features with learned representations and reports sizable gains on some sarcasm variants, but the marginal and statistically insignificant benefit of adding gaze to the text CNN, reliance on small eye‑tracking datasets, and lack of released code/strong theoretical justification make this an incremental rather than highly novel contribution. | B(3): From my perspective as a cognitive-NLP empiricist, the paper contributes a moderately novel engineering step by moving from handcrafted gaze features to end-to-end CNN-based learning of cognitive features for sentiment and sarcasm tasks—this is a useful practical advance over the Mishra et al. systems that relied on manual feature design. However, the problem (sentiment/sarcasm classification) is well-studied and the methodological shift to a dual-input CNN is an incremental, non-trivial extension rather than a fundamentally new idea. The small and sometimes statistically insignificant gains (e.g., only a 1.36% nonsignificant improvement for sarcasm) and use of existing gaze datasets limit the perceived originality, so I rate it as moderate novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a standard CNN architecture to jointly learn representations from text and eye‑movement (gaze) signals, which is a logical extension of prior handcrafted‑feature work (Mishra et al.) rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. It does clearly differentiate itself from prior work by replacing handcrafted gaze/text features with learned representations and reports sizable gains on some sarcasm variants, but the marginal and statistically insignificant benefit of adding gaze to the text CNN, reliance on small eye‑tracking datasets, and lack of released code/strong theoretical justification make this an incremental rather than highly novel contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: From my perspective as a cognitive-NLP empiricist, the paper contributes a moderately novel engineering step by moving from handcrafted gaze features to end-to-end CNN-based learning of cognitive features for sentiment and sarcasm tasks—this is a useful practical advance over the Mishra et al. systems that relied on manual feature design. However, the problem (sentiment/sarcasm classification) is well-studied and the methodological shift to a dual-input CNN is an incremental, non-trivial extension rather than a fundamentally new idea. The small and sometimes statistically insignificant gains (e.g., only a 1.36% nonsignificant improvement for sarcasm) and use of existing gaze datasets limit the perceived originality, so I rate it as moderate novelty.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical progression from motivation to method and experiments, and its main contributions are stated early (introducing a CNN framework to learn gaze-derived cognitive features). Notation and methodological details (including hyperparameters) are reported and related work coverage is adequate, and the writing quality is described as good; however some experimental details—such as presentation of figures/tables and the communication of small, statistically insignificant gains (e.g., the 1.36% sarcasm improvement)—could be clearer to help readers assess practical importance. Overall the presentation is strong but could benefit from slightly more explicit emphasis on result interpretation and clearer visual/illustrative materials. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem to method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (CNN framework to learn cognitive features from gaze for sentiment and sarcasm) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and sufficient for reproduction, and experimental results (accuracy numbers and % improvements) are presented clearly. Figures/tables and examples effectively support the claims (e.g., reported accuracy gains and limitations), and the writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity or grammatical issues. Aspects like minor presentation polish or expanded visualization could further improve clarity, hence a 4 rather than a 5.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a logical progression from motivation to method and experiments, and its main contributions are stated early (introducing a CNN framework to learn gaze-derived cognitive features). Notation and methodological details (including hyperparameters) are reported and related work coverage is adequate, and the writing quality is described as good; however some experimental details—such as presentation of figures/tables and the communication of small, statistically insignificant gains (e.g., the 1.36% sarcasm improvement)—could be clearer to help readers assess practical importance. Overall the presentation is strong but could benefit from slightly more explicit emphasis on result interpretation and clearer visual/illustrative materials.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem to method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (CNN framework to learn cognitive features from gaze for sentiment and sarcasm) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and sufficient for reproduction, and experimental results (accuracy numbers and % improvements) are presented clearly. Figures/tables and examples effectively support the claims (e.g., reported accuracy gains and limitations), and the writing quality is good with minimal ambiguity or grammatical issues. Aspects like minor presentation polish or expanded visualization could further improve clarity, hence a 4 rather than a 5.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical results (notably the large sarcasm gain) provide evidence that CNN-learned gaze features can help classification, but they also flag important methodological weaknesses: small datasets and few readers, limited reporting of hyperparameter search and statistical testing, and lack of reproducibility artifacts. These gaps undermine confidence in the generality and robustness of the claims, so a moderate score reflecting supported but not fully sound/rigorous evidence is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main empirical claim—that a CNN can learn useful cognitive (gaze) features and improve sentiment/sarcasm classification—is partially supported by experiments showing up to an 11.27% gain on sarcasm and modest gains when adding gaze to text. However, the evaluation raises methodological concerns: datasets are small (≈1k examples) with gaze from only 5–7 readers, hyperparameters were set by trial-and-error, code is not reported, and statistical testing is either missing or only briefly noted (the reported 1.36% gain is stated as statistically insignificant). These gaps (limited data, lack of rigorous hyperparameter search/reproducibility, and weak/insignificant gains for the main multimodal claim) prevent me from rating the work higher despite reasonable baseline comparisons.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's central claims—that a CNN can learn cognitive (gaze) features and that combining text and learned gaze features improves classification—are supported by experiments on two eye-tracking datasets (Mishra et al. 2016a and Joshi et al. 2014) and comparisons to prior handcrafted-feature baselines; the reported large gain on sarcasm (up to 11.27%) and modest gains when adding gaze are concrete experimental results. There is no indication of mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup uses relevant baselines, though evaluation relies solely on accuracy and the paper acknowledges one small improvement is statistically insignificant; it is unclear whether broader statistical testing was performed across all comparisons. Given these minor methodological gaps (limited reporting on significance testing and hyperparameter tuning done by trial-and-error), the technical claims are largely supported but not flawless.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is on-topic and of interest to a top-tier audience for exploring gaze-based multimodal signals, but both raise concerns about modest empirical gains, small datasets/few readers, and limited ablation/hyperparameter rigor. Given these shared weaknesses outweigh the contributions—despite some reported improvements and use of existing eye-tracking datasets—I assign a borderline appropriate score of 3 to reflect interest but insufficient empirical robustness for a stronger recommendation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an NLP/ML-relevant problem (sentiment and sarcasm detection) and explores an interesting multimodal signal (gaze) so the topic and audience fit top venues. However, the empirical gains are small or statistically insignificant (e.g., only 1.36% for sarcasm), datasets are tiny with few readers per snippet, and the method depends on costly eye‑tracking data without clear broad utility or rigorous ablation; hyperparameters were set by trial-and-error and code availability is not mentioned. Given these issues the work is borderline appropriate: of interest to the community but lacking the empirical rigor and broader impact typically required for a top-tier acceptance.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits well within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it introduces a multimodal CNN that learns cognitive (gaze) features for sentiment and sarcasm — a topic of clear interest to the community that values human behavioral signals. The use of two eye-tracking datasets (Mishra et al., 2016a and Joshi et al., 2014) and reported gains (notably up to 11.27% improvement over prior sarcasm baselines) show practical empirical contribution. My only minor concern is that the gaze-only benefit for the CNN is small and statistically insignificant for sarcasm (1.36%), and dataset sizes/readers are modest, leaving questions about generality; nonetheless the paper targets the right audience and evaluation standards for the venue.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem and introduces a promising automated approach to learn cognitive features from gaze, but impact is limited by small datasets, few readers, lack of code, and only modest or statistically insignificant gains when adding gaze to text models. Given these merits and clear limitations, the work is a useful moderate contribution that opens avenues for future work rather than a field-changing result, so a mid-level score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a relevant task (sentiment and sarcasm detection) and explores an interesting cognitive signal (gaze), but the demonstrated benefits are narrow and fragile: the CNN variants beat prior handcrafted systems on sarcasm by at most 11.27% while adding gaze to text CNN yields only a 1.36% (statistically insignificant) gain. The approach depends on small datasets with eye-tracking from few readers and offers no code, limiting reproducibility and practical uptake; without stronger, repeatable gains or theoretical justification for why the CNN should extract useful cognitive features from sparse gaze traces, I view the impact as limited and niche rather than field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important and timely area—leveraging eye-tracking (gaze) signals for sentiment and sarcasm detection—and contributes a practical CNN framework that automatically learns cognitive features rather than relying on handcrafted ones. From my cognitive-NLP perspective this is valuable: demonstrating improved performance (CNN variants beating prior systems, up to 11.27% on sarcasm) and modest gains when adding gaze to text models shows promise for integrating behavioral signals. However, impact is tempered by small datasets (994 and 843 snippets, few readers per snippet) and only marginal/statistically insignificant improvement from gaze on the key sarcasm task, limiting immediate uptake. Overall the work opens useful avenues for automated cognitive-feature learning but is a moderate rather than transformative contribution.

main_claims: Introduce a CNN-based framework that automatically learns cognitive features from eye-movement (gaze) data and text for sentiment polarity and sarcasm detection.

Using a combination of automatically learned text and gaze features yields better classification performance than (i) CNN systems using text alone and (ii) existing systems that rely on handcrafted gaze and textual features.
CNN model variants outperform traditional systems on the sarcasm detection task by up to 11.27% (and adding gaze to text CNN gives a small 1.36% improvement which is statistically insignificant).
_summary.methodology: A Convolutional Neural Network with two components processes text and gaze (eye-movement) inputs to automatically learn feature representations which are used jointly to classify sentiment polarity and sarcasm.
datasets: Dataset 1 (Mishra et al., 2016a): 994 snippets (383 positive, 611 negative), 350 sarcastic; eye-movement data from 7 readers per snippet.
Dataset 2 (Joshi et al., 2014): 843 snippets (443 positive, 400 negative); eye-movement data from 5 readers per snippet.
baselines: Mishra et al. (2016c) systems that rely on handcrafted text and gaze features (for sentiment).
Mishra et al. (2016b) sarcasm classifier (for sarcasm detection).
limitations: Improvement from adding gaze component to CNN for sarcasm detection is only 1.36% and is statistically insignificant.
Model hyper-parameters were fixed by trial and error and tuning of hyperparameters is left for future work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - sentiment analysis and sarcasm detection using cognitive (gaze) data

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning and computational linguistics with a focus on model interpretability and theoretical guarantees for learning algorithms.
traits: Penalises papers lacking theoretical justification or analysis: expects explanation of why the proposed architecture should capture the target signal and, where possible, formal or empirical ablation supporting claims about representation capacity.

Requires thorough experimental controls: deducts points for missing ablations, unclear baselines, or reliance on statistically insignificant improvements (e.g., downgrades claims when improvements are <2% or not tested for significance).

Skeptical of costly auxiliary data: penalises methods that rely on expensive-to-collect modalities (eye-tracking) unless they provide strong, repeatable benefits or clear transfer/usefulness beyond the specific dataset.
Favors reproducibility: deducts if code, model details, hyperparameters, or dataset access are not fully provided.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on whether the paper provides principled justification and rigorous empirical controls; I downgrade work that appears incremental, under-validated, or dependent on expensive auxiliary signals without clear benefit.

reviewer_b.name: Cognitive-NLP Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in cognitive NLP and multimodal machine learning who develops and evaluates models that incorporate human behavioral signals (eye gaze, EEG) for language understanding tasks.
traits: Rewards use of human cognitive signals: gives positive credit when papers leverage gaze/behavioral data and demonstrate how such signals improve downstream NLP tasks or provide new insights.

Values empirical contributions and practical systems: rewards strong dataset engineering, novel multimodal architectures, and end-to-end models that reduce reliance on handcrafted features even when gains are modest.

Awards impact for new modalities/datasets: scores higher if a paper publishes or documents collection/processing of a new cognitive dataset and shows plausible utility for related tasks (transferability, interpretability).
Accepts small but consistent empirical improvements: willing to credit +1–3% gains and qualitative analysis, especially when the addition is from a novel modality or produces interpretable behaviors.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I emphasize empirical novelty and practical impact of incorporating cognitive signals; I tend to reward well-executed multimodal systems and dataset/architecture contributions even if theoretical analysis is limited.
